text: Hello, I'm sharing a robotic right hemicolectomy for a proximal transverse colon cancer.

The surgeon begins a medial to lateral dissection beneath the ileocolic pedicle using a standard setup with hockey stick ports positioned along the left side, utilizing two instruments in the left hand and one in the right hand.
The surgeon approaches the anterior surface of the superior mesenteric vein during the medial to lateral dissection beneath the iliocolic pedicle.
The surgeon has identified the ileocolic vessels during the robotic right hemicolectomy, with visible anatomical structures and surrounding tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon exposes the superior mesenteric artery, located anterior to the superior mesenteric vein, revealing its position on the left side of the vein.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum, ensuring it is cleared before proceeding to encircle the ileocolic vessels.
The surgeon carefully inspects the junction of the ileocolic vein and the superior mesenteric vein to ensure there is no injury and to confirm a high ligation.
The surgeon divides the ileocolic artery after further dissecting it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the anterior surface of the superior mesenteric vessels, maintaining close proximity to these structures during the procedure.
The surgeon marks the planned line of transection on the colon to ensure precise cutting and avoid inadvertently extending towards the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon isolates the right colic vessels, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the right colic vein and right colic artery, preparing to isolate and dissect these vessels.
The surgeon lifts the mesentery off the head of the pancreas, revealing branches of the loop of Henle while continuing dissection along the superior mesenteric vessels.
The surgeon isolates a vessel near the anterior surface of the superior mesenteric vessels and prepares to transect it.
The surgeon marks the planned line of transection, ensuring precision in the separation of the tissue near the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon identifies a tattoo marking approximately six centimeters distal to the tumor, indicating the planned site for surgical intervention.
The surgeon attempts to excise the entire tattooed area, which is marked as six centimeters distal to the tumor, ensuring complete removal during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon marks the line of planned transection extending to the distal end of the tattoo and attempts to isolate the right branch of the middle colic artery.
The surgeon thins out the fatty tissue overlying the isolated vessel, carefully removing excess adipose tissue to improve visibility.
The surgeon creates a window around the right branch of the middle colic vessels, carefully isolating these structures from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon continues to thin out the fatty tissue surrounding the right branch of the middle colic vessels using a vessel sealer, effectively performing the task without switching back to scissors.
The surgeon identifies an enlarged lymph node at the base of the middle colic vessels and excises it. The lymph node is visually distinct from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon isolates the right branch of the middle colic artery and vein, sequentially ligating and transecting the artery followed by the vein.
The surgeon advances along the transverse colon mesentery, approaching the colon wall, following the thinning of the fatty tissue overlying the right branch of the middle colic vessels.
The surgeon separates mesogastric fat from the undersurface of the transverse colon mesentery, lifting the tissue to improve visibility and access.
The surgeon sweeps down the tissue, revealing what appears to be the marginal vessel, and continues dissection towards the colon wall.
The duodenum is visible in the surgical field, positioned adjacent to the transverse colon mesentery.
The surgeon places a sponge underneath the duodenum to protect it during the medial dissection through the lesser sac.
The surgeon identifies and dissects the omentum fused above the liver, resecting it towards the specimen side where the tumor is located.
The surgeon enters the lesser sac to mobilize the hepatic flexure, joining the previously dissected medial space. The pylorus is visible as the tissue is carefully dissected up against it.

This gentleman had a rather high hepatic flexure. I usually don't change the positioning of the bed, but you'll see as I get a little bit higher and lose my visualization, although edited out, if you look down at the bottom at the altimeter, I will change from reverse Trendelenburg to a little bit more supine to help this tissue with gravity dependently fall away.
The surgeon delivers additional omentum, revealing more hepatocolic fat. The hepatic flexure is visible beneath the liver, extending outward and around.
The surgeon approaches the surgical site from the inferior aspect, adjusting the angle to improve visualization and access to the target area.
The surgeon lifts the appendix off the retroperitoneum and continues the dissection along the white line to connect with the previously dissected superior area.
The surgeon completes the dissection and proceeds to take the small bowel mesentery, visually separating it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) to check perfusion and prepares to staple through the suprapubic port, which will be upsized for extraction. The small bowel is divided using the stapler, with other ports measuring eight millimeters.

The surgeon lines up the isoparastaltic anastomosis, placing two stay sutures—one proximal and one distal. The distal suture stabilizes the bowel, while the proximal suture aids in positioning the stapler. The surgeon holds the bowel inferiorly to guide it onto the stapler without direct manipulation.

The surgeon cuts out an obstructing tissue to close the common channel, using a 3-0, 6-inch relock suture. An inner baseball stitch is applied from the near corner to the far corner, followed by a return stitch.
And that concludes the operation. The specimen was extracted through the suprapubic port site. The patient did well and was discharged on postoperative day one.
